stem: Consider \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \). This function is one-to-one on the interval \( (-\infty, k] \), but on no larger interval of that form. What is \( k \)?
solution: A parabola is one-to-one exactly on each side of its vertex, whose \( x \)-coordinate is \( x = -\dfrac{[[b]]}{2\cdot[[a]]} = [[answer]] \). So \( f \) is one-to-one on \( (-\infty, [[answer]]] \) and on no larger such interval, giving \( k = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of 2 in the vertex formula \( -\frac{b}{2a} \).
This flips the sign of the vertex \( x \)-coordinate.
This uses \( 4a \) in the denominator instead of \( 2a \).
This gives the minimum value (the vertex \( y \)-coordinate), not its \( x \)-coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Consider \( f(x) = |[[a]]x - ([[b]])| + ([[c]]) \). This function is one-to-one on the interval \( (-\infty, k] \), but on no larger interval of that form. What is \( k \)?
solution: A V-shaped absolute value graph is one-to-one exactly on each side of its vertex, which occurs where the inside expression is zero: \( [[a]]x - [[b]] = 0 \Rightarrow x = \dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \). So \( f \) is one-to-one on \( (-\infty, [[answer]]] \) and on no larger such interval.
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the vertex \( x \)-coordinate.
This inverts the fraction, dividing \( a \) by \( b \) instead of solving for \( x \).
This multiplies the coefficients instead of dividing to solve for \( x \).
This reports the vertical shift \( [[c]] \), which does not affect the vertex's \( x \)-coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The function \( f(x) = (2a - ([[m]]))x + ([[b]]) \) fails to be one-to-one for exactly one real value of the parameter \( a \). What is that value?
solution: A linear function fails to be one-to-one exactly when its slope is zero: \( 2a - [[m]] = 0 \), so \( a = \dfrac{[[m]]}{2} = [[answer]] \). For every other value of \( a \), \( f \) has nonzero slope and is one-to-one.
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 2 when solving \( 2a = [[m]] \) for \( a \).
This doubles the correct value instead of halving \( [[m]] \).
This flips the sign of the correct value.
This reports the constant term \( [[b]] \), which never affects one-to-one-ness of a line.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The function \( f(x) = \dfrac{[[a]]x + ([[b]])}{[[c]]x + d} \) collapses to a constant function (and so is nowhere one-to-one) for exactly one value of \( d \). What is that value?
solution: A linear-fractional function is constant exactly when its numerator and denominator are proportional, i.e. \( [[a]]\cdot d = ([[b]])\cdot([[c]]) \), so \( d = \dfrac{([[b]])([[c]])}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \). For every other \( d \), \( f \) is one-to-one on its domain.
distractor_rationales: This swaps which coefficients are multiplied and which is divided, inverting the proportionality condition.
This inverts the correct fraction entirely.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
This omits the coefficient \( [[a]] \) from the proportionality condition entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = \begin{cases} [[a1]]x + ([[b1]]) & \text{if } x < [[t]] \\ [[a2]]x + k & \text{if } x \ge [[t]] \end{cases} \), where both pieces are strictly increasing. For \( f \) to be continuous at \( x = [[t]] \) (which guarantees \( f \) is one-to-one on all of \( \mathbb{R} \)), what must \( k \) equal?
solution: Continuity at \( x=[[t]] \) requires the two pieces to agree there: \( [[a1]]([[t]]) + ([[b1]]) = [[a2]]([[t]]) + k \), so \( k = [[a1]]([[t]]) + ([[b1]]) - [[a2]]([[t]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the left piece's value at \( [[t]] \) directly without solving for \( k \) in the right piece.
This flips the sign of the correct combination.
This reports only the left piece's constant term, ignoring both slopes and the breakpoint \( [[t]] \).
This adds where it should subtract the right piece's slope contribution.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
